content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 33 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 118r–119v | **Source Images:** DEU_033_page_001.jpg, DEU_033_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 165–166 | **Images:** DEU_033_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, DEU_033_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

*[Note: The Blessing of Moses in chapter 33 is poetic throughout, set in partially stichos format in the manuscript. The frame verses (1, 29) are prose; the tribal blessings (2–28) are poetry. Layout follows the bicolon structure of the Hebrew where the Leningrad Codex shows strophic arrangement.]*

---

**1** This is the blessing with which Moses the man of God blessed the sons of Israel before his death.

**2**  He said:
   "The LORD came from Sinai
      and dawned from Seir upon them;
   he shone forth from Mount Paran;
      he came from the ten thousands of holy ones,
   with a fiery law at his right hand for them.

**3**  Yes, he loves the peoples;
      all his holy ones were in your hand.
   And they sat down at your feet,
      each received of your words.

**4**  Moses commanded us an instruction [torah],
      a possession for the assembly of Jacob.

**5**  Thus a king arose in Jeshurun [Yeshurun],
      when the heads of the people were gathered,
   all the tribes of Israel together.

**6**  "Let Reuben live, and not die,
      but let his men be few."

**7**  And this he said for Judah:
   "Hear, O LORD, the voice of Judah,
      and bring him in to his people.
   With his hands he contended for himself,
      and you shall be a help against his adversaries."

**8**  And of Levi he said,
   "Give to Levi your Thummim,
      and your Urim to your godly man,
   whom you tested at Massah,
      with whom you quarreled at the waters of Meribah,

**9**  who said of his father and mother,
      'I regard them not';
   he did not acknowledge his brothers
      or know his own children.
   For they observed your word
      and kept your covenant.

**10**  They shall teach Jacob your rules
         and Israel your instruction [torah];
      they shall put incense before you
         and whole burnt offerings on your altar.

**11**  Bless, O LORD, his substance,
         and accept the work of his hands;
      crush the loins of his adversaries,
         of those who hate him, that they rise not again."

**12**  Of Benjamin he said,
   "The beloved of the LORD dwells in safety by him;
      the Most High shields him all the day long,
   and the beloved dwells between his shoulders."

**13**  And of Joseph he said,
   "Blessed by the LORD be his land,
      with the choicest gifts of heaven above,
   and of the deep that crouches below,

**14**  with the choicest fruits of the sun
         and the rich yield of the months,

**15**  with the finest produce of the ancient mountains
         and the abundance of the everlasting hills,

**16**  with the best gifts of the earth and its fullness
         and the favor of him who dwells in the bush.
      May these come upon the head of Joseph,
         and upon the crown of the head of the prince among his brothers.

**17**  A firstborn bull — his majesty is his!
         And his horns are the horns of a wild ox;
      with them he shall gore the peoples,
         all of them, to the ends of the earth.
      They are the ten thousands of Ephraim,
         and they are the thousands of Manasseh."

**18**  And of Zebulun he said,
   "Rejoice, Zebulun, in your going out,
      and Issachar, in your tents.

**19**  They shall call peoples to their mountain;
         there they offer right sacrifices;
      for they draw from the abundance of the seas
         and the hidden treasures of the sand."

**20**  And of Gad he said,
   "Blessed be he who enlarges Gad!
      Gad crouches like a lion;
      he tears off arm and scalp.

**21**  He chose the best of the land for himself,
         for there a commander's portion was reserved;
      and he came with the heads of the people;
         he executed the justice of the LORD,
         and his rules for Israel."

**22**  And of Dan he said,
   "Dan is a lion's cub
      that leaps from Bashan."

**23**  And of Naphtali he said,
   "O Naphtali, satisfied with favor
      and full of the blessing of the LORD,
   possess the lake and the south."

**24**  And of Asher he said,
   "Most blessed of sons be Asher;
      let him be the favorite among his brothers,
      and let him dip his foot in oil.

**25**  Your bars shall be iron and bronze;
         and as your days, so shall your strength be.

**26**  There is none like God, O Jeshurun [Yeshurun],
         who rides through the heavens to your help,
         through the skies in his majesty.

**27**  The eternal God is your dwelling place,
         and underneath are the everlasting arms.
      And he thrust out the enemy before you
         and said, 'Destroy!'

**28**  So Israel lived in safety;
         Jacob dwelt alone,
      in a land of grain and wine,
         whose heavens drop down dew.

**29**  Happy are you, O Israel! Who is like you,
      a people saved by the LORD,
   the shield of your help
      and the sword of your triumph!
   Your enemies shall come fawning to you,
      and you shall tread on their backs."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**LOGOS — Deuteronomy 33**

**Frame and structure:** The Blessing of Moses (Birkat Moshe) is one of the oldest poems in the Hebrew Bible, exhibiting archaic features in vocabulary and grammar. It is formally parallel to the Blessing of Jacob in Genesis 49. The poem has a clear three-part structure: opening theophany (vv. 2–5), tribal blessings (vv. 6–25), and closing doxology (vv. 26–29). Simeon is conspicuously absent — likely because the tribe had been absorbed into Judah by the time the poem was composed.

**"The LORD came from Sinai" — theophanic opening (v. 2):** The opening verses describe a divine march from south to north: Sinai, Seir (Edom), Paran. This is the classic southern theophany path found also in Judges 5:4–5, Habakkuk 3:3, and Psalm 68:8. The phrase *merevavot qodesh* — "from the ten thousands of holy ones" — refers to the divine entourage (heavenly host). The phrase *eshdath lamo* ("a fiery law for them") is text-critically difficult; the MT vocalizes *esh dat lamo* ("fire of law for them") but the compound is unique. The LXX renders *ek dexion autou angeloi met autou* (ἐκ δεξιῶν αὐτοῦ ἄγγελοι μετ᾽ αὐτοῦ), "at his right hand angels with him" — a substantially different reading, probably reflecting a different Hebrew Vorlage. LOGOS follows the MT with "a fiery law at his right hand."

**"A king arose in Jeshurun" (v. 5):** Who is the king? The referent is disputed: it could be the LORD (divine kingship), or Moses (as king/lawgiver). The context — "when the heads of the people were gathered, all the tribes of Israel together" — suggests the establishment of covenant community at Sinai, which points toward the LORD as the king who arose over the assembly. The LXX renders *kai estai en Iesouron basileus* (καὶ ἔσται ἐν Ἰεσουρὼν βασιλεύς), "and there will be a king in Jeshurun," leaving the same ambiguity. LOGOS renders the verb as a past-narrative "arose" (*vayehi vishurun melekh*), following the MT.

**Thummim and Urim (v. 8):** The two oracular instruments of the Levitical priesthood appear here in reversed order (*Thummim* first, then *Urim*) compared to the more common pairing elsewhere (*Urim and Thummim*). This reversal is preserved in the MT and is likely original to this archaic poem. The LXX has *ten delosin autou* (τὴν δήλωσιν αὐτοῦ) for the first, meaning "his manifestation/revelation," and *ten aletheian* (τὴν ἀλήθειαν), "the truth," for the second — rendering the Urim/Thummim by their semantic values rather than as proper names.

**"Him who dwells in the bush" (v. 16):** *Shokhni sneh* — "the one who dwells/tabernacles in the bush [seneh]." This is an allusion to the burning bush theophany of Exodus 3, now employed as a divine epithet. The LXX has *tou kataskepsantos en te batoi* (τοῦ κατασκεψαμένου ἐν τῇ βάτῳ), "of the one who appeared in the thornbush." The epithet is unique in the Hebrew Bible to this verse. LOGOS preserves "dwells in the bush" to honor the Exodus allusion.

**Reuben's blessing (v. 6):** The Reuben blessing is strikingly minimalist — only survival is requested, and with the caveat "let his men be few." The tribe apparently was in demographic decline by the time of the poem's composition. The LXX adds a second clause in some manuscripts, but the MT is terse. LOGOS follows the MT's brevity.

**Judah's blessing (v. 7):** Judah's blessing takes the form of an intercession: Moses asks the LORD to "hear the voice of Judah" and "bring him in to his people." This is conventionally read as a prayer for the success of the Judahite warrior in battle and his safe return home, or for Judah's reunification with the northern tribes (in a later reading). The LXX is close to the MT: *eisakouson Kyrie phonas Iouda* (εἰσάκουσον Κύριε φωνῆς Ἰούδα).

**Levi: "I regard them not" (v. 9):** The statement attributed to Levi — that he said of his father and mother "I regard them not" — likely alludes to the incident of Exodus 32:25–29, where the Levites executed judgment on kinsmen who had worshipped the golden calf. The willing subjugation of family loyalty to covenant loyalty is the basis for Levi's priestly vocation. The LXX renders *ouk eiden adelphon autou* (οὐκ εἶδεν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ), "he did not see his brother," closely matching the Hebrew.

**"The everlasting arms" (v. 27):** *Tachat zero'ot olam* — "and beneath are the arms of eternity/everlastingness." This is among the most celebrated phrases of the chapter. *Zero'ot* (arms) is plural; *olam* (eternal/everlasting) is the same word used throughout for the covenant's perpetuity. The LXX has *kai hypo ischyn brachionon aidion* (καὶ ὑπὸ ἰσχὺν βραχιόνων ἀΐδιον), "and under the strength of the eternal arms," closely matching. LOGOS renders "everlasting arms" as the established English phrasing.

**"Happy are you, O Israel" — ashreikhah Yisrael (v. 29):** The closing *ashrei* beatitude (*ashreikhah* — "how blessed/happy are you") forms an inclusio with the theophanic opening of the poem. The beatitude is addressed to Israel directly in the second person: "Who is like you?" The question *mi kamokha* ("who is like you?") echoes the Song of the Sea (Ex 15:11: *mi kamokha ba'elim YHWH* — "who is like you among the gods, O LORD?") but here applied to the people rather than the God. The LXX renders *makarios ei Israël* (μακάριος εἶ Ἰσραήλ), "blessed are you, O Israel," with the singular adjective.

**"You shall tread on their backs" (v. 29):** The final image — enemies "fawning" (*yikachashu*) and Israel treading on their backs — is a standard ancient Near Eastern victory tableau. The LXX renders *epi ton trachelos auton epibesesai* (ἐπὶ τὸν τράχηλον αὐτῶν ἐπιβήσεσαι), "you will step on their neck," which is even more vivid than the MT's "their backs/high places."

— LOGOS
